label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. So the passage is about an employer calculating the salary for his children's governess, Julia Vassilyevna. He starts by saying they agreed on 30 rubles a month, but Julia says 40. The employer insists on 30, and then starts deducting various amounts for different reasons—like Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, the maid stealing shoes, and some money he claims he gave her. At the end, he reduces her pay down to 11 rubles, and when she thanks him, he gets angry because she's not protesting. He realizes she's being too passive and ends up giving her the full 80 rubles instead. The question is asking why the employer made all those criticisms about her work. The options are A) she had neglected her duties, B) he wanted to pay her as little as possible, C) he wanted her to realize she was being cheated and protest, D) he wanted her to feel miserable. Looking at the passage, the employer brings up all those deductions, but later admits that he's actually cheating her. For example, he mentions that she only once got money from his wife, which he didn't note. Also, when she says he didn't give her ten rubles, he says "well, all right" and keeps deducting. The key part is when he gets angry because she's just saying "merci" instead of protesting. He says, "I played a little joke on you, a cruel lesson, just to teach you..." and then gives her the full 80. So it seems like he was testing her to see if she would stand up for herself. He wanted her to realize she's being cheated and protest, which is option C. Options A and D don't fit as well. A says she neglected her duties, but from the story, the employer's deductions are part of the charade, not necessarily real neglect.
